title: On the K-theory of Z/p^n
speaker: Achim Krause
abstract: In recent work with Antieau and Nikolaus we use prismatic cohomology to compute algebraic K-theory of Z/pn and similar rings. Our approach is based on a new description of absolute prismatic cohomology, which can be made completely algorithmic in this case. In this talk, I want to give an overview over these methods.
tags: Special Year Workshop on p-adic Arithmetic Geometry

proof_stages.label: Reduction from K-theory to syntomic homology
proof_stages.summary: For p-complete finite rings such as Z/p^n and O_K/pi^n, the cyclotomic trace identifies p-completed K-theory with TC in nonnegative degrees, and the motivic filtration plus cohomological concentration reduces the homotopy groups to individual syntomic homology groups.

proof_stages.label: Computable relative prismatic cohomology via prisms
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Bhatt-Scholze absolute and relative prismatic cohomology, delta rings and prisms, and the universal prismatic-envelope description that makes relative prismatic cohomology algebraically computable for regular-sequence quotients.

proof_stages.label: Extension from prisms to delta pairs
proof_stages.summary: The relative theory is refined to a functor on delta pairs (A,R), independent of a chosen distinguished prism ideal, recovering both absolute prismatic cohomology over the initial delta ring and usual Bhatt-Scholze relative prismatic cohomology when a prism structure exists.

proof_stages.label: Breuil-Kisin descent diagram for absolute prismatic cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Using a Liu-Wang-inspired descent pattern, absolute prismatic cohomology with Nygaard filtration is expressed as a limit of relative Breuil-Kisin-type prismatic complexes in several variables, where the delta-pair formalism resolves the ambiguity from multiple possible Eisenstein distinguished elements.

proof_stages.label: Two-term syntomic square and finite computation
proof_stages.summary: Cohomological dimension bounds truncate the cosimplicial complex to a square involving can-minus-Frobenius maps and a connection-like differential, while z-adic filtered delta-pair structures give bounded filtrations that reduce syntomic and K-theory computations to finite linear algebra and imply even-degree vanishing in high weights.

proof_stages.label: Origin of the connection from one-coskeletal descent
proof_stages.summary: A one-coskeletal cosimplicial ring diagram, equivalently a Hopf algebroid/groupoid object, yields differential operators by dualizing the degree-one term; in the Breuil-Kisin case an explicit presentation and completed monomial basis define the operator nabla and show it satisfies a Leibniz-type rule with subtle correction terms.

examples.name: q-de Rham or q-prism contrast
examples.note: The q-prism setting is used as a contrasting related case where connection formulas may look nicer but the q-1-adic filtration lacks the Frobenius growth property needed for the finite computations.
examples.engagement_reason: the q-prism contrast is analyzed by checking why the q or q−1 filtrations fail under Frobenius, e.g. phi(q−1)=q^p−1 has only one factor of q−1

passes.reason: standard q-prism/q-de Rham setting used as a contrast where the desired Frobenius-compatible filtration fails

examples.name: K_41 of Z/32
examples.note: The concrete finite group K_41(Z/32) is mentioned as a typical explicit output of the computational method, mainly to contrast raw computation with the conceptual question of the connection.
examples.engagement_reason: K_41 of Z/32 is only invoked as an example of a computation one might care about, with no actual group computation shown

examples.name: Divided-power Taylor groupoid
examples.note: The power-series Hopf-algebroid with divided powers of z1-z0 is worked out to recover Taylor's formula and ordinary derivatives, indicating how dual basis elements should be interpreted as connection-like operators when divided powers are deformed or absent.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the divided-power two-variable setup, identifies the dual basis, and derives the Taylor/nth-derivative formula from it

passes.reason: standard divided-power formal Taylor groupoid used as a model for Taylor/differential operators, not as a refutation
The divided-power Taylor groupoid is used as a standard regular model for Taylor operations, not to refute a claim.
